objective: clear lead vocal with safe feedback margin
steps: Set preamp so the loudest chorus peaks cleanly before the clip light threatens.
Trim rumble first, then pull muddy low mids before adding any vocal presence.
Use a short hall reverb sparingly so the singer feels support without washing out diction.
Solve feedback with mic position and speaker placement before chasing more gain.
Leave a little headroom for louder songs or crowd-noise spikes.
notes: Reliable starting scene for compact rooms where clarity matters more than size.

target: Drum bus in a hybrid live-record setup
steps: Keep the dry drum bus as the reference and build the compressed return underneath it.
Use a faster attack only if cymbals or snare crack are jumping too far forward.
Push the compressed return until the kit feels denser, then pull it back before the groove loses lift.
High-pass unnecessary low-end from the parallel path so kick weight stays intentional.
Check the blend in mono to make sure the excitement survives without smearing the image.
notes: Parallel compression should feel like the kit got more confident, not smaller.

constructLabel: Vocal chain basics for new sessions
objective: shape a modern vocal path without over-processing
steps: Clean up the raw take before reaching for tone, especially breaths, clicks, or obvious plosives.
Use subtractive EQ before compression so the compressor is not reacting to rumble or mud.
Set the first compressor for control and the second only if the vocal still jumps out unevenly.
Add saturation or color after stability is under control, not as a fix for balance problems.
Leave delay and reverb sends low until the lyric and center image already feel solid.
notes: A good vocal chain solves level and tone in stages instead of stacking dramatic moves too early.

steps: Confirm source power, cabling, and input assignment before touching gain or EQ.
Check that every channel reaches the main bus and any required monitor sends.
Verify outboard, matrix, or speaker processing paths in the same order every show.
Use solo or metering to prove where signal stops instead of guessing from silence.
Finish with a talkback or playback pass through the full path so the whole system is proven.
notes: Signal flow discipline saves more shows than clever EQ ever will.

target: Input gain decisions across a live mix
objective: protect headroom and keep the mix responsive
steps: Set input gain from the loudest expected performance level, not the quiet rehearsal version.
Leave room for excited moments before adding compressor makeup or fader gain.
Avoid solving audibility with preamp gain when the issue is really routing or monitor balance.
Keep outputs and speakers in a healthy operating range before forcing the inputs hotter.
Recheck gain after major tone or preset changes because those moves can shift perceived level.
notes: Gain staging is the first reliability move because every later decision depends on it.

target: Ring-out for vocal mics and wedges
objective: increase usable level without wrecking tone
steps: Start with speaker placement and mic direction before you cut anything.
Bring the system up gradually and catch the first repeatable ring, not random room splash.
Use the narrowest cut that solves the problem and stop before the vocal gets hollow.
Re-test after each cut because feedback points move once the system changes.
Save the ring-out only after performers are on stage and monitor positions are final.
notes: Feedback control is a sequence problem more than an EQ problem.

target: Basic monitor send strategy for a 4-piece band
objective: give each player only what supports performance
steps: Build the lead vocal first because it usually anchors timing and pitch for the room.
Add the player's own instrument only until they stop fighting for it.
Keep low-end sources conservative in wedges unless someone truly cannot perform without them.
Use short language during line check so performers answer with useful yes or no feedback.
Revisit monitor balance after the first loud song because stage volume usually changes once the room fills.
notes: A helpful monitor mix feels uncluttered and specific, not impressive on paper.

target: Lead vocal lost in a dense band mix
objective: restore intelligibility without making the vocal harsh

context.source: lead vocal in a band mix

steps: Check arrangement masking first, especially guitars, keys, or cymbal wash occupying the same range.
Cut competing low mids in supporting channels before boosting the vocal again.
Add presence in small moves and stop as soon as consonants become clear.
Re-check effects because too much verb or delay can hide the vocal more than EQ can fix it.
Listen from the audience area if possible because vocal balance often lies differently than it does at the console.
notes: The cleanest vocal fix is often subtractive work somewhere else in the mix.

target: Scene recall on a busy multi-band day
objective: load fast without bringing back unsafe assumptions
steps: Treat scenes as a starting snapshot, not as proof that the current patch or gain is correct.
Confirm phantom, gain, and output routing every changeover before trusting the loaded scene.
Name scenes with band and context so the wrong recall stands out immediately.
Keep a minimal fallback scene for emergencies when a band file behaves unpredictably.
After the show, fold the useful improvements back into the master templates instead of letting drift accumulate.
notes: Scene recall is fastest when it supports your checklist instead of replacing it.

target: Speech system for a conference room panel
objective: keep voices even and intelligible across the room
steps: Set mics for clear speech first and keep playback below the moderator by default.
Trim low-end buildup that comes from table mics or room boundaries before adding presence.
Keep effects effectively dry because clarity is more valuable than polish in this room.
Walk the room and listen for coverage gaps instead of trusting only the console position.
Save a version with known-good speech routing so the room can come back quickly next time.
notes: Conference work is won by consistency, coverage, and low-stress operation.

target: Reference check after long mix sessions
objective: regain perspective before making bigger decisions
steps: Drop monitoring level and listen through the song top to bottom without touching anything first.
Switch to one familiar reference track to recalibrate tonal balance and vocal level expectations.
Note only the three most obvious issues before reopening the session for fixes.
Check mono or a single small speaker briefly so center balance problems reveal themselves.
Leave detailed automation or surgery for the next pass once your ears are reset.
notes: Perspective resets prevent tired ears from turning a good mix into a complicated one.

objective: support inconsistent singers without losing intelligibility
steps: Keep the preamp conservative so sudden loud singers still have headroom.
Start flat, cut mud before boosting highs, and avoid over-shaping for any one voice.
Use a short vocal reverb or delay for confidence, but keep the tail controlled.
Keep speakers well in front of the microphone and watch for cupped-mic feedback.
Favor consistency over perfection because the singer changes every song.
notes: This preset is for rotation and survivability, not for dialing in one perfect vocalist.
